critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-structured and shows awareness of common pitfalls. However, there is one fundamental flaw that undermines the central validity claim, and two secondary issues worth noting.

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage via resolution notes in the text features**

This is the most serious problem. The text features include resolution notes — text written *after* the ticket is closed. The FCR label is derived from the same closure event and the 30-day follow-up window. The model trained on full ticket text including resolution notes is therefore learning from text that is generated contemporaneously with or after the ground-truth outcome is determined.

The claim being made is that the text classifier can predict FCR failure. The implicit prerequisite is that the features available at prediction time are causally or temporally prior to the outcome. Resolution notes violate this: they are written by the agent as part of, or after, the resolution act. An agent closing a ticket they know will be reopened may write different notes than one closing a ticket they believe is resolved. The model may be learning to read resolution notes as outcome-encoded artifacts rather than predictors of the outcome.

What makes this particularly insidious is that the methodology explicitly acknowledges including resolution notes ('the model is evaluated on the same complete interaction record that is available for downstream audit and quality workflows') and frames this as a feature, not a bug. But 'available for audit' and 'available for prediction' are different use cases with different timing constraints. If the goal is runtime FCR triage (routing, escalation), resolution notes do not exist at decision time. If the goal is retrospective quality audit, the framing of 'prediction' is wrong.

The evidence for leakage would be: (a) ablating resolution notes from the text features and checking whether AUROC drops substantially; (b) inspecting model attention or feature attribution to see if resolution-note tokens dominate; (c) checking whether AUROC on tickets with short/absent resolution notes is markedly lower. If the model's advantage over the baseline disappears when resolution notes are excluded, the reported AUROC gain is an artifact of outcome-adjacent text, not of predictive linguistic signal in the conversation itself.

**Issue 2: Temporal split strategy — stratified random vs. time-based**

The methodology uses stratified random sampling over the full 24-month corpus. This means the training set contains tickets from the same calendar period as the test set. Any temporal autocorrelation (e.g., tickets from the same product launch wave, the same agent cohort's tenure, the same seasonal pattern) will inflate measured generalization. More concretely: if an agent appears in both training and test, the model learns that agent's individual FCR propensity and recovers it at test time — this looks like generalization but is really memorized individual-level signal.

A time-based split (train on months 1–18, validate on 19–21, test on 22–24) would test whether the learned signal generalizes forward in time, which is the operationally relevant question. The stated justification — that stratification avoids a 'single unusually easy or difficult window dominating' — is a reason to stratify *within* a temporal split (to ensure product area and tier coverage across all periods), not a reason to abandon temporal ordering entirely.

Evidence: compare AUROC from the stratified split vs. a time-based split. If AUROC drops materially under temporal ordering, the stratified result is overly optimistic. A particularly informative diagnostic is to check whether agent-level FCR rate (already a feature) explains most of the gap between the two splits.

**Issue 3: Asymmetric hyperparameter treatment of baseline**

The baseline is fixed at C=1 while the full model undergoes a 5-point grid search. The methodology justifies this as keeping 'the validation budget focused on the representation choice,' but the effect is that any AUROC gap could partially reflect the full model's tuned regularization rather than the text features per se. A logistic regression on high-dimensional mean-pooled embeddings is sensitive to regularization strength; the optimal C for the text-augmented feature space may be very different from C=1 for the metadata-only space.

The right fix is either (a) run the same grid search on the baseline, or (b) run an ablation: train the full model at C=1 and compare to baseline at C=1, treating the tuned version as a sensitivity check. Without this, the design conflates 'better representation' with 'better-tuned regularization.'

Evidence: train baseline with the same grid search; if its AUROC at optimal C is meaningfully higher than at C=1, part of the reported gap is due to tuning asymmetry.

**Summary**

Issue 1 (resolution note leakage) is a fundamental validity threat: it could explain most or all of the text model's advantage and would mean the reported AUROC measures something other than FCR prediction. Issues 2 and 3 are compounding biases that inflate the measured advantage. The methodology could be salvaged by (a) excluding resolution notes or treating them as a separate ablation condition, (b) using a temporal split, and (c) applying the same hyperparameter search to both branches.
all_issues_raised: Resolution notes are included in text features but are written contemporaneously with or after ticket closure, creating outcome-adjacent signal that is unavailable at genuine prediction time and may encode the FCR outcome directly.

Stratified random sampling across the full 24-month corpus allows training and test tickets to be temporally interleaved, inflating AUROC by enabling memorization of agent- and account-level patterns rather than testing forward generalization.

The baseline model is fixed at C=1 while the full model undergoes a regularization grid search, conflating the effect of text features with the effect of better-tuned regularization and biasing the comparison in favor of the full model.

Stratified random sampling across the full 24-month corpus allows training and test tickets to be temporally interleaved, inflating AUROC by enabling memorization of agent- and account-level patterns rather than testing forward generalization.

The baseline model is fixed at C=1 while the full model undergoes a regularization grid search, conflating the effect of text features with the effect of better-tuned regularization and biasing the comparison in favor of the full model.
